key_insight: In nanoscratch of 316H stainless steel, martensitic transformation follows a pressure-and-shear-controlled sequence: γ-austenite transforms to ε-martensite under hydrostatic compression, then to α′ where sliding-induced shear relaxes constraint.
engineering_relevance: Understanding how contact stress states select transformation pathways helps explain tribolayer formation and galling resistance in stainless and hardfacing alloys, informing surface design for wear-critical components.
method: In-situ synchrotron X-ray nanodiffractometry during nanoscratch testing
Finite element modelling to interpret local stress/strain fields
Spatial mapping of evolving γ, ε, and α′ phase distributions
evidence_signal: The abstract reports spatially resolved experimental maps of strain and martensitic phase evolution beneath a single asperity, supported by FEM interpretation of the observed distributions.
watch_item: Results are based on a single-asperity nanoscratch and in-situ synchrotron setup, so translating the pathway selection directly to real multi-asperity service contacts may require caution.
paper_title: Spatially resolved in-situ characterisation of competing martensitic transformation pathways during nanoscratch in 316H Stainless Steel
